This is insane.🤯

Claude Code users don’t even know this exists yet.

You’re still asking
“which model codes better?”

That’s outdated.

The real move = make them compete.

OpenAI just dropped an official Codex plugin for Claude Code —
and now you can call Codex inside Claude.

This isn’t a feature.
It’s a workflow upgrade.

3 commands. All lethal:

→ /codex:review
Scans uncommitted changes or diffs.
Clean. Fast. No noise.

→ /codex:adversarial-review
This is where it gets serious.
It questions your decisions:

“Why this caching?”
“Race condition?”
“What breaks at scale?”

You can control how aggressive it gets.

→ /codex:rescue
Claude stuck?
Hand it over to Codex mid-task.
--resume continues exactly where it stopped.

And then the real weapon: Review Gate

Claude finishes → Codex auto-reviews →
Finds issues? It blocks.

No silent bugs. No guesswork.

For anything high-risk:

• Auth
• Infra scripts
• DB migrations
• Data-sensitive logic

Adversarial mode = unfair edge.

Claude writes.
Codex challenges everything.

That tension builds better code.

Stop choosing models.

Make them fight.

quoted_tweet.text:
Codex App 才是最适合普通人上手的 AI 工具。

功能全面、界面简单易懂。不用一开始就折腾终端和命令行，也能轻松覆盖学习、工作和 vibe coding 等常见场景。

入门 Codex App 必须了解的五件事:

1、左侧栏工作区分 Projects 和 Chats，结构清晰。 Projects 对应真实文件夹，适合按项目管理问答、文件和任务；Chats 用来记录零散问答。
小 tip：快捷键 Cmd+G 可快速搜索聊天会话

2、支持多任务并行处理。你可以同时推进不同项目，并在左侧栏清楚掌握任务进度和状态。

3、Plugins包含外部工具和skill。外部工具指 GitHub、Notion、Gmail 这类第三方工具的接入；Skill指可复用的工作流，自己创建或用别人现成的都行。

4、Automations 支持将重复性任务设成定时自动执行。

5、内置终端、浏览器、文件预览功能，一个 App 满足所有需求，不用到处切换。

While alternative coding harnesses may have short term lift, they will be bitter lesson’d away. I am bearish on any harness that doesn’t come from the lab whose model you are using. You’re fighting against post-training.

To put a finer point on this, you know how like, ioctls are like “huh that's weird but I guess whatever it's what we've got we can work with that”? It is exact the same with like, the particular JSON construction the Codex shell tool uses.

The model used to mangle nested quotes in this monstrosity RPC all the time but now it does not and it does not matter that the API is bad because billions of failed invocations are used to train to the harness we have, not the harness we deserve.

quoted_tweet.text:
Ilya Sutskever just told the AI industry why scaling is finished.

One word built it. One word is about to break it.

Sutskever: “Scaling is just one word, but it’s such a powerful word because it informs people what to do.”

For five years, that single word replaced an entire research culture.

Nobody needed breakthroughs. They needed bigger checks.

Sutskever: “If you mix some compute with some data into a neural net of a certain size, you will get results, and you will know that it will be better if you just scale the recipe up.”

That’s not science. That’s a recipe.

Sutskever: “Companies love this because it gives you a very low risk way of investing your resources.”

The most transformative technology in human history ran on the same logic used to franchise a restaurant chain.

More locations. More ingredients. Same recipe. Predictable returns.

You didn’t need researchers who could see around corners.

You needed accountants who could approve purchase orders.

But recipes expire.

Sutskever: “At some point though, pre-training will run out of data. The data is very clearly finite.”

Five years of infrastructure. Five years of hiring. Five years of investor decks.

All built on top of something temporary.

Sutskever: “I don’t think that’s true.”

The co-founder of OpenAI. The mind behind the breakthroughs that made this entire era possible.

Saying more money won’t solve it.

Sutskever: “In some sense we are back to the age of research.”

Most of the companies racing to build AGI were never research companies.

They were scaling companies.

They hired for execution. Not discovery. They optimized for throughput. Not insight.

The talent pipelines. The investor pitches. The board decks.

All built around one assumption.

That the recipe would never expire.

It’s expiring.

And the companies that spent five years perfecting the art of spending money are about to discover something.

The next era demands what capital can’t purchase.

An original idea.

kept.text:
Sam Altman is basically saying OpenAI does not want to be just another high-margin software company selling AI tools.

The bigger ambition is to become the core intelligence infrastructure layer for the economy.

i.e. OpenAI wants to sit underneath companies, products, workflows, agents, consumer apps, internal automation systems, developer tools, and business processes. Not just as a chatbot. Not just as an API. 

But as something closer to an “intelligence meter”, where people and companies consume AI the way they consume electricity, cloud compute, or internet bandwidth.

The key point is about margin philosophy.

Altman is saying that AI may not remain a very high-margin business forever. As models get smarter, switching from 1 AI to another becomes easier. A company can ask an agent to migrate code, replace workflows, test alternatives, and move faster than before. So defensibility may not come from locking customers into a single app.

It may come from becoming the cheapest, most useful, most reliable intelligence utility at massive scale.

That is a very different OpenAI strategy than “build the best AI app and charge premium prices.”

The real strategy sounds more like, that OpenAI wants to align itself with the success of the whole economy. If companies automate more, build more, sell more, ship faster, and create new products using OpenAI’s intelligence layer, then OpenAI grows with them.

This is closer to an infrastructure business than a normal software business.

The important part is that Altman seems comfortable with OpenAI becoming a huge low-margin company, as long as it becomes deeply embedded in global economic activity. That is a very Amazon Web Services-style idea, but for intelligence instead of cloud servers.

So in the future the winning AI company may not the one with the fattest margins, but the one that becomes the default meter for intelligence usage across the world.

---

article.preview_text:
Like all good articles, this one is a reaction to low-effort engagement bait. This was the winner this time:
 
The main issue is that public indices often measure headline benchmark performance, not

kept.text:
You cannot tell if what you are reading was written by a human.

Neither can your doctor. Neither can your professor. Neither can the peer reviewer who approved the scientific paper you just cited.

A systematic review published in April 2026 — screening 22,541 records from 2025 and 2026 — just synthesized every credible study on whether humans can detect AI-generated content. Text. Images. Voice. All of it.
Human detection accuracy varied widely but generally clustered around chance performance. Overall, the literature shows that humans are generally unreliable detectors of gen AI content.

 The same accuracy you would get by flipping a coin.
The paper is called "Is It Cake or Is It AI?" Published by a researcher at Penn State's Department of Health Policy. 30 studies included. Every study from 2025 and 2026 only — meaning this is the most current, most relevant assessment of human detection ability that exists.
Here is what the individual studies found.

A study published in Nature in March 2026 found that six academics — each with up to two years of experience — were asked to identify AI-written dental research abstracts. Accuracy ranged from 44% to 76%. The overall finding: relying on human judgment alone is insufficient for identifying AI-assisted academic text. OpenAI
44% accuracy. That is worse than a coin flip. A random guess would have scored 50%.

A separate study involving five blinded human raters across five experimental conditions — human-written, lightly AI-edited, heavily AI-edited, AI-written from human content, and fully AI-generated — found something even more alarming.

Human scoring accuracy was uniformly low, with an overall accuracy of 19%, indistinguishable from chance. OpenAI
19%. Not 50%. Not 44%. Nineteen percent. Worse than random guessing on a binary task.

And the tools built to help humans detect AI are not doing much better.
More than a dozen elite universities have disabled AI detection entirely. OpenAI shut down its own detector after it correctly identified AI text just 26% of the time. Johns Hopkins University's guidance to faculty is now: "No products on the market can effectively identify generative AI."

The university that runs one of the world's most respected medical schools — Johns Hopkins — has officially told its faculty that AI detection does not work.

Think about what is already passing through systems that depend on human verification.
Medical research papers reviewed by academics who score 19% on AI detection. Legal briefs reviewed by judges who cannot tell AI-written arguments from human ones. Job applications screened by hiring managers who cannot distinguish AI-generated cover letters from authentic ones. News articles read by editors trained to catch fabrication — but not trained to catch this kind.
The systematic review ends with a question that no institution has answered.

If humans are generally unreliable detectors of AI content, what does this mean for how we evaluate or trust content at all? Vellum
The answer, so far, is silence.

Source: Ramos · Penn State · "Is It Cake or Is It AI?" · arXiv:2604.03437 · April 2026 · https://t.co/QWpGgbPBzc
Cheng et al. · "Ability of AI Detection Tools and Humans" · November 2025 · https://t.co/RUGQro5KsF

kept.text:
1500亿美元，马斯克把 OpenAI 的命运拖进法庭。

这场加州庭审，第一周就让他作证三天。
总时长超过 7 小时。
争的不是一家公司值多少钱。
争的是 OpenAI 当年到底被许诺成什么。

马斯克的版本是，他捐了 3800 万美元，还亲自帮 OpenAI 起步。
名字是他想的。
关键人是他招的。
早期资金是他给的。
连 Ilya Sutskever 这种 Google 顶尖研究员，也是他拉进来的。

他还把自己和微软、NVIDIA CEO 的关系搬上台面。
AI 最缺什么？
算力。
马斯克认为，OpenAI 早期能拿到关键资源，离不开他的信用和人脉。

OpenAI 的版本完全相反。
他们认为马斯克真正想要的是控制权。
2018 年离开董事会之后，OpenAI 越做越大，他才对这家公司今天的成功心有不甘。

最刺眼的矛盾在这里。
马斯克反复把 OpenAI 描述成慈善机构。
但 2015 年 OpenAI 成立时那篇博客里，charity 这个词一次都没出现。

法庭上，他留下了一句最核心的指控：
“你不能偷走一个慈善机构。”

OpenAI 的解释也很现实。
要买算力。
要付顶尖科学家的薪水。
要接受私人投资。
所以才需要由非营利组织监管的营利实体。

问题是，AI 这门生意太烧钱了。
当初用“安全、开放、造福人类”召集人才和捐款。
后来又必须靠资本、算力和高薪把模型推到前线。
理想和公司结构撞在一起，火花就从实验室烧到了法庭。

还有一个细节很有意思。
OpenAI 律师追问马斯克是否读过奥尔特曼转发的转型条款清单。
马斯克承认，他没读细则，只看了标题。

这句话对陪审团很关键。
如果他知道转型安排，背叛叙事就会被削弱。
如果他只是被标题安抚，OpenAI 的道德压力就会变大。

奥尔特曼预计还要出庭自辩。
马斯克要的不只是 1500 亿美元赔偿。
他还要求 OpenAI 恢复为非营利组织，并让奥尔特曼离开董事会和 CEO 位置。

赔偿所得将流向 OpenAI 的慈善部门。
这让整件事更像一场关于 AI 原罪的审判。

硅谷最贵的东西，可能从来不是模型。
是创始时写下的那几句承诺。

kept.text:
Is AI the same as the dot-com bubble?

Are we going to see another global crash and economy reset?

Let's see what numbers say 👇

➤ NVIDIA is valued at $4.3 trillion, stock up 1300% since 2022.

➤ OpenAI raised $110 billion in a single round at a $730 billion valuation, the largest private funding round in history.

➤ AI startups attracted $258 billion in 2025 alone, that's 61% of ALL global VC money flowing into one sector.

➤ The top 5 companies now hold 30% of the entire S&P 500, the greatest concentration in half a century.

🚨 54% of fund managers openly call AI stocks a bubble.

Now here's where it gets scary:

Microsoft funds OpenAI:
OpenAI buys compute from CoreWeave, CoreWeave leases GPUs from NVIDIA, financed again by Microsoft and Oracle. The same dollar recycled across balance sheets, not organic growth, a circular liquidity loop.

And all of this is happening while the US national debt just crossed $39 trillion.

Debt-to-GDP hit 100% for the first time since World War II, and the US is now spending more on interest payments ($1 trillion in 2026) than it spends on national defense.

AI-related investment currently accounts for half of US GDP growth. If AI spending pulls back, GDP growth slows, debt-to-GDP gets worse, and the entire system starts compounding against itself.

The dot-com bubble didn't burst because the internet failed, it burst because valuations assumed it would replace gravity.

Nasdaq lost 78% before we got Amazon and Google out the other side.

AI is real, the technology is transformative, but the economy sitting underneath it is standing on $39 trillion of debt with a trillion dollar interest bill.

What side are you on.

If you have a bunch of extremely fragmented and messy tasks, your own personal energy will most likely be completely drained by a single Claude code window,

you'll feel like opening three or four Claude codes, switching back and forth, monitoring status—it's absolutely going to drive you insane,

and if you have a few extremely massive ideas, you set up the goal with goal-driven, set up the criteria, and each task is a relatively long one,

https://t.co/mJld9XcBjp

then you'll be perfectly at ease assigning the tasks to a master agent, letting each master agent automatically manage 1~10 subagents under them, working in 3~30 hour cycles,

at that point, you can open 5~7 Claude code windows with a clear conscience, enjoying a state where your attention isn't scattered, where you can stay relatively focused while working relatively in parallel.

Because you trust that these 5~7 Claude code windows don't need real-time monitoring—goal-driven will automatically verify, provide feedback, and continue for you, and it operates on 3~30 hour cycles,

then you can hand things over to these 5~7 Claude code windows with total peace of mind and perfect justification, without needing to monitor them at very high frequency, and no one will disrupt your current focus.

I keep telling you this over and over,

OpenClaw gave you subagents with multiple depths, but most people don't even know how to use them,

Claude code gave you subagent capabilities, but a whole bunch of stuff like agency-agent wrongly abstracts them into a company—that's completely missing the point.

You are electricity, you are light, you are the one true myth—you must start from your own energy and attention to design a management mode like goal-driven,

only then can you completely free yourself from the intense complexity, internal friction, chaos, and tedium of context switching.

Just like Kim Jong-un managing North Korea.

kept.text:
The OpenAI marketing strategy the last month has been genius

It's also been what America has needed

They've leaned all the way into AI optimism and fun

80% of Americans hate AI. While a lot of AI leaders get on TV and say every job will be eliminated and we'll all go broke and die, OpenAI has rightly leaned into EMPOWERMENT instead of destruction

And it's true. AI is all about empowerment. It is not a doomsday technology. It won't eliminate all developer jobs. It's a technology that allows ANYONE to build literally any idea they can think of.

Is there immense economic disruption happening right now? Yes. Is it because of AI? No.

In 2020 a lot of companies decided to spend recklessly as the entire world printed trillions of dollars. 

Unfortunately blaming the economic disruption on AI is an easy way for CEOs to excuse their outrageous spending mistakes while still boosting the stock price. It's a chicken shit move that has led to most Americans despising AI

While irresponsible CEO's blame layoffs on technology, we have people in the government telling everyone that data centers are somehow using up all our water. An absolutely insane, false, and quite honestly laughable idea.

If our leadership, from the government, to the CEO's, to the content creators leaned into the truth, which is AI is a radically positive and empowering technology and stopped spreading fear to excuse bad decisions and for clicks, the sentiment in America would quickly turn around

Which is what we need, because China has 80%+ positive sentiment about AI. (We have 80%+ negative sentiment in America)

If we continue being negative while China continues being positive, we will 100% lose our position as a global power

AI is the great equalizer. It's a beautiful technology that allows ANYONE to build any life they want.

If we want to win this existential race, we need to start acting accordingly

kept.text:
How the original OpenAI donations were structured.

1. OpenAI Inc. was registered as a 501(c)(3) non-profit in 2015.
2. Stated mission: ensure AGI "benefits all of humanity," with research published openly.
3. Donors, including Musk, contributed under that mission with no equity, no return, no claim.
4. In 2019, OpenAI created a capped-profit subsidiary. The cap is now functionally irrelevant given current valuations.
5. The non-profit board still technically governs, but the capped-profit entity is where the money and the people live.

The legal question: did the structural shift breach the terms under which donations were made.

How the original OpenAI donations were structured.

1. OpenAI Inc. was registered as a 501(c)(3) non-profit in 2015.
2. Stated mission: ensure AGI "benefits all of humanity," with research published openly.
3. Donors, including Musk, contributed under that mission with no equity, no return, no claim.
4. In 2019, OpenAI created a capped-profit subsidiary. The cap is now functionally irrelevant given current valuations.
5. The non-profit board still technically governs, but the capped-profit entity is where the money and the people live.

The legal question: did the structural shift breach the terms under which donations were made.

I drop the architecture.

holaOS is a mech bus, not a bot.

Opus 4.7 at the top. Five sub-mechs under it. Each trained to break the others, not agree.

"why not Codex under the hood"

Codex writes code. 4.7 reads behavior. Different surface. You don't send a compiler to a knife fight.

"why five mechs"

Four is symmetric. Six starts voting. Five falsifies clean.

Whale imbalance > 1.7x

444 trades

$200 -> $32,480 in 26 days

I did. Found the stack myself.

He goes offline. Node keeps printing.

kept.text:
imagine you're an early stage employee at openai or anthropic with a fat equity payout coming at IPO this year

you've already scrolled zillow to buy a house for your parents, and bookmarked a sportscar for yourself

things are great. but there's one worry: what if markets turn before IPO and your stock actually tanks on day one

then you hear about a revolutionary new pre-IPO product on hyperliquid

you can hedge by shorting a synthetic version of your equity and locking in a sale price before the actual IPO

sounds too good to be true

but you do your research and convince yourself it's a great idea. it's a permissionless product, so there's no way you can get caught insider trading

the trade is simple: short your company's stock in the pre-IPO perp to lock in a sale price now. when the IPO actually happens, close your short and sell the real stock to settle the trade

only catch: you need to put up a lot of margin to open the short safely

so you take out a 7 fig bank loan and move it through three crypto exchanges to cover your tracks

you open the short, officially "lock in" your sale proceeds, and voila.. you just outsmarted and frontran 99% of your coworkers

things are great. now all you need is patience until IPO day, when you can close the trade

the price actually starts dropping in the weeks leading up to IPO

all the more validation that you made the smart move. you can only feel bad for your coworkers who'll get a worse selling price at IPO than you

things are going great and you start sketching out your post-IPO retirement phase

5 days before the IPO, you wake up and can't seem to find your position on hyperliquid

weird.. maybe it's a UI bug?

you check the hyperliquid discord for support. the channels are buzzing with people complaining about a scam wick on a pre-IPO market

you ask, naively, what happened to your short and where the margin went

that's when someone drops a news article on you:

"at 4am EST today, a wallet started aggressively market buying pre-IPO stock on hyperliquid, pumping its price by 271% in 38 minutes and liquidated every short position on the market. the wallet walked away with $47m in profits from liquidating shorts"

what? your short got closed? but where did the margin go?

that can't be true. you did your research. you asked opus 4.7 to plan this trade out for a hypothetical scenario. this has to be a fake headline

you open twitter. that's all anyone is talking about. everyone mocking the people who shorted the pre-IPO stock

confused about what this means for you, you start frantically googling:

"wtf is a scam wick?"
"what is a liquidation in perps?"
"how can i get my money back after getting liquidated on hyperliquid?"

then it hits. you've lost the entire loan by getting liquidated. the same loan you were going to repay with your IPO sale proceeds

and now the stock price is down and your pre-IPO short is liquidated. selling all your real equity at IPO won't even cover what you owe the bank

from a 7 figure payday at IPO to a 7 figure debt situation. it took you years to pay off your student loan. this hole is orders of magnitude deeper

you call in sick to work. tell your gf there's a family emergency at home and you need to be back home for a week

you can only see one way out of this mess

Supplement the Codex system prompt with your own custom instructions.

- providing your name
- negative prompts (em dashes, common phrases, etc) 
- personalization / personality

Did you know you can customize the pre compaction prompt for custom instructions?

If you are have some custom workflows that would benefit from explicit steering, you can prompt the compaction model directly.

You can also do this via a separate markdown file with experimental_compact_prompt_file (string - path).

Both of these features are configured in your config.toml file,  and work for both CLI and Codex App.

🚨 Sam Altman (OpenAI Ceo) just said agents sdk 2.0 is underrated

5,159 people saw it

maybe 10 actually know what he meant

most people using agents sdk 2.0 wrong

they build one agent, give it a bunch of tools, and wonder why it breaks on complex tasks

that's not how you're supposed to use it
the real power is handoffs

one agent does its job then passes the task to a specialist agent mid-conversation

no human input needed

think of it like a company

you don't hire one person to do sales, legal, and engineering

you hire specialists and they hand off to each other

agents sdk 2.0 lets you build that exact structure in code

here's what a real setup looks like

you have a triage agent that reads the user request
it decides which specialist handles it

the specialist completes the task

if it needs another skill it hands off again

the whole thing runs without you touching it

now here's what to actually use alongside it

> https://t.co/oGwVlfcv1J - start here, the docs are actually good
> https://t.co/mBOvPuOt6o - gives your agents 200+ real tool integrations in minutes
> https://t.co/Od9CE9CZkV - lets your agents run code safely in sandboxed environments
> https://t.co/zlv0P8iUUJ - adds persistent memory so your agents remember context across sessions

> https://t.co/SiV4Gc8dDG - lets your agents browse the web like a real person

the mistake everyone makes is trying to make one agent do everything

split the work, use handoffs, and your agent goes from toy to actually useful

most people won't figure this out for another 6 months
you just did
